workflowtools 0.0.13
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Tracking input data with a manifest
  • Changelog

Tracking input data with a manifest

Alex M. Chubaty

Source: vignettes/input-data-manifest.Rmd
input-data-manifest.Rmd

workflowtools ships an input-data manifest API for projects that consume multiple datasets from heterogeneous sources (BC Data Catalogue, NRCan, ERA5, hand-curated files). The manifest is a single JSON file that records, for each dataset:

  • its source (catalogue URL, query, retrieval timestamp),
  • its vintage (publisher’s version or last-modified date),
  • a local pointer plus content hash,
  • and a structured citation (BibTeX key, full entry, prose).

Downstream tooling (e.g. a per-PDF provenance appendix or a data-sources.bib sidecar) reads the manifest to populate itself, so the project’s input documentation stays in sync with what was actually fetched.

This vignette walks through the typical lifecycle.

library(workflowtools)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'workflowtools'
#> The following object is masked from 'package:utils':
#> 
#>     timestamp

1. Where does the manifest live?

Convention: a single JSON file at data/input_manifest.json, alongside the project’s data/ directory. The default arguments to every manifest function point there, so most calls don’t need to mention the path.

For this vignette we’ll use a temp file.

manifest_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "input_manifest.json")

2. Register the first input – a hand-written record

For sources without a dedicated extractor (a USB-stick drop, an internal reference layer, an ERA5 batch download), build a record manually with input_manifest_record():

nfdb <- input_manifest_record(
  id = "nfdb",
  name = "Canadian National Fire Database (NFDB) -- Agency Fire Data",
  source = list(
    type = "http_download",
    url = "https://cwfis.cfs.nrcan.gc.ca/en_CA/nfdb"
  ),
  local_path = "data/processed/nfdb_poly.gpkg",
  version_or_vintage = "2024-12-01",
  license = "OGL-Canada-2.0",
  citation = list(
    bibtex_key = "CFS:NFDB",
    formatted_text = "Canadian Forest Service (2024). Canadian National Fire Database -- Agency Fire Data."
  )
)
register_input(nfdb, manifest_path)

register_input() is idempotent and keyed by id: re-running with an updated nfdb record replaces the existing entry rather than duplicating it.

nfdb$version_or_vintage <- "2025-01-15"
register_input(nfdb, manifest_path)

read_input_manifest(manifest_path)[[1]]$version_or_vintage
#> [1] "2025-01-15"

3. Register a BC Data Catalogue record automatically

For datasets that flow through the BC Data Catalogue, metadata_bcdata() fetches the catalogue record, extracts the relevant fields, caches them, and returns a manifest-ready record:

vri <- metadata_bcdata("2ebb35d8-c82f-4a17-9c96-612ac3532d55")
register_input(vri, manifest_path)

The cache (default location: tools::R_user_dir("workflowtools", which = "cache")/metadata/) means a second call returns immediately without hitting the network. Cache TTL is 30 days by default; override with metadata_bcdata(..., ttl_days = 90) or options(workflowtools.metadata_cache.ttl_days = 90). Pass refresh = TRUE to force a fetch.

Behind the scenes metadata_bcdata() calls bcdata::bcdc_get_record() and dispatches its result to the S3 method extract_metadata.bcdc_record(). You can call those directly if you want to short-circuit the cache:

bcdata::bcdc_get_record("...") |> extract_metadata()

4. Register a raw URL with the fallback method

For an arbitrary URL with no dedicated metadata API, extract_metadata.character() does a best-effort HTTP HEAD to populate the record’s vintage from Last-Modified:

era5_doc <- extract_metadata(
  "https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/cdsapp#!/dataset/reanalysis-era5-single-levels",
  id = "era5-single-levels"
)
register_input(era5_doc, manifest_path)

Pass head_lookup = FALSE to skip the HEAD request (useful when offline or when the server is known to reject HEAD).

5. Extending: a custom S3 method

If you have a source not covered by the built-in methods, define an S3 method for extract_metadata. Here’s a sketch for a hypothetical NRCan FBP fuel-type record:

extract_metadata.nrcan_fbp_record <- function(x, id = NULL, ...) {
  input_manifest_record(
    id        = id %||% "cffdrs-fbp-fuels-2024",
    name      = x$dataset_title,
    source    = list(type = "nrcan_fbp", url = x$portal_url),
    local_path = file.path("data", x$file_name),
    license   = x$license,
    citation  = list(
      bibtex_key = sprintf("NRCan:%s", x$version_year),
      formatted_text = sprintf("NRCan (%s). %s.", x$version_year, x$dataset_title)
    )
  )
}

Subsequent calls to extract_metadata(record) dispatch on class(record)[1L] and your method takes over.

6. Sync citations to a sidecar BibTeX file

The bridge to a project’s bibliography is sync_manifest_to_bibtex(): a one-way, conservative exporter that writes eligible manifest citations to a sidecar .bib, deduplicated against the project’s hand-curated bibliography.

references_bib <- file.path(tempdir(), "references.bib")
out_bib        <- file.path(tempdir(), "data-sources.bib")

## A hand-curated references.bib that already contains an entry for one of
## the manifest records; the sync should skip it (and warn) to keep the
## curated bib the source of truth for that key.
writeLines("@misc{CFS:NFDB,\n  title = {Curated NFDB entry},\n}", references_bib)

result <- sync_manifest_to_bibtex(
  manifest       = manifest_path,
  out            = out_bib,
  references_bib = references_bib
)
#> Warning: 1 manifest record(s) skipped due to key collision with /tmp/RtmpPu112S/references.bib: CFS:NFDB.
#>   Mark these records with citation.external = TRUE to suppress this warning.
result
#> $written
#> [1] 0
#> 
#> $skipped_external
#> [1] 0
#> 
#> $skipped_collision
#> [1] "CFS:NFDB"

The collision warning is a deliberate prompt: either delete the duplicate entry from references.bib (letting the sync own the key from now on) or mark the manifest record with citation = list(..., external = TRUE) to silence the warning and keep the curated entry authoritative.

Records with citation = list(external = TRUE) are skipped silently:

nfdb$citation$external <- TRUE
register_input(nfdb, manifest_path)
sync_manifest_to_bibtex(manifest_path, out_bib, references_bib)

7. Reading the manifest from downstream consumers

Provenance appendices, audit tables, and bibliography pre-renders all consume the manifest via read_input_manifest():

recs <- read_input_manifest(manifest_path)
data.frame(
  id      = vapply(recs, `[[`, character(1L), "id"),
  vintage = vapply(recs, function(r) r$version_or_vintage %||% "", character(1L))
)
#>     id    vintage
#> 1 nfdb 2025-01-15

For prose output, [citation_text()] and [as_bibentry()] handle records one at a time.

8. Recommended project wiring

A typical project arranges things like this:

  1. Each input-fetch target in your pipeline (targets, drake, Make, …) calls register_input(extract_metadata(source_obj), manifest_path) after the fetch succeeds.
  2. A final target calls sync_manifest_to_bibtex() once per build.
  3. The qmd / Rmd report’s bibliography lists both the curated and auto-generated .bib files.
  4. A “provenance appendix” R chunk reads the manifest and renders a kable of inputs with their vintages and citations.

The result: every report ships with a self-describing record of where its data came from – without a hand-maintained input list to keep in sync.

See also

  • [input_manifest_schema()] for the canonical field list.
  • [extract_metadata()] for the S3 generic + the methods shipped with this package (character, bcdc_record).
  • [sync_manifest_to_bibtex()] for the dedup rules.

Developed by Alex M Chubaty.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
